retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Sinus Fracture(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Per the Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) protocol, the evaluation of trauma patients begins with an assessment of the patient's airway, respiratory capacity, and circulatory status. While assessing the extent of the patient's disabilities, a thorough neurologic exam should be performed to calculate a Glasgow Coma Scale Score, assessing cranial nerve function, and inspecting for focal deficits or bony injury to the calvarium. After ensuring patient stability and obtaining a patient history, it is important to perform a full head and neck examination. The exact order of this exam will vary from practitioner to practitioner. However, every exam should be performed in the same order to ensure nothing is missed. The provider should inspect the entire head and neck for any lesions, abrasions, contusions, or active bleeding. Since many MSFs are associated with multiple maxillofacial bone fractures, one of the most important roles of this secondary survey is to look for injuries not noticed on the primary survey. Practitioners should pay attention to obvious deformities, most commonly involving the nose, zygomatic arch, and mandible. [7] Bleeding from lacerations should be controlled, and the wounds should be irrigated, explored, and closed if possible. The facial bones should be palpated to evaluate bony step-offs or instability. A complete oral examination should be performed as there may be protrusion of the bony maxilla or mandible through the mucosa and into the oral cavity. Also, look for any loose teeth or changes in occlusion, which can be the only indication of a mandible fracture. [7] Though nasal examination may be limited, it is important to examine for cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leaks, septal hematomas, epistaxis, or purulent drainage. Examination of the ears can reveal Battle's sign, auricular hematomas, CSF otorrhea, or hemotympanum, suggesting further fracture involvement of the skull base. [8] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Fracture -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who have sustained a Lefort I fracture classically present with mobility of the tooth-bearing segment of the maxilla, altered occlusion, or damage to the teeth. [10] The maxilla is often floppy and free-floating when the fracture is complete and easy to manipulate, and very obvious if the patient tries to talk. Lefort II fractures have a pyramidal shape fracture pattern, and patients may present with orbital involvement, including orbital hematoma, epistaxis, and possibly cerebral spinal fluid rhinorrhea. Often the inferior orbital rims are affected, and fractures of the orbits are present. [10] Letort III fractures involve nasal bones, lateral orbital walls, pterygoid processes, and zygomatic arches. Patients may present with complete separation of the midface and the cranium or orbital complications. [10] Patients who have sustained zygomatic-maxillary complex fractures may experience edema, subconjunctival hemorrhage, periorbital edema, and enophthalmos. [11] Four percent to 12% of ZMC fractures result in major ocular injuries and minor ocular injuries are seen in 63% of patients. A detailed eye assessment by an ophthalmologic consultant should be considered if you suspect any injuries, including open globe, lens dislocation, retrobulbar hematoma, or hyphema. [12] An expert tip in conducting this assessment is to use your fingers and hands to wiggle and manipulate bony segments. It may be slightly uncomfortable for the patient, but it is the best way, combined with a computed tomography scan, to diagnose facial fractures, particularly of the maxilla and mandible.

retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The acute management of the patient is vital, and this may require the resuscitation team, including a cricothyroidotomy or tracheostomy to secure the airway. Midface fractures are a relative contraindication to nasal intubation, and these patients will eventually need an airway that does not go between the teeth to allow for reconstruction. However, with glide scopes and fiberoptic scopes, some anesthesia providers are comfortable nasally intubating these patients. Another option is a submental intubation when done with a surgical team. Essentially, the patient is orally intubated, an incision is made under the chin in the submental area, and the tube pulled through.

retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Fracture -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: When a patient presents with a suspected maxillary fracture, immediate assessment of the patient is key, as there may be concurrent life-threatening injuries. A primary survey of the patient is needed, including an advanced trauma life support (ATLS) assessment. The resuscitation team may be required in the immediate management of the patient. Once the patient is in a stable condition, a thorough history of the trauma is needed. It is important to ascertain if the patient lost consciousness or has any other signs of head injury, as neurological care would be a priority. Care must be taken to note the time the patient last ate, and their medications, in case surgery is indicated. A secondary survey of the patient should be carried out to assess the trauma and for other concurrent injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Operative Management
retrieved_docs.content: The indications for operative management of zygomatic arch (ZA) and ZMC fractures are to restore the form and function of the midface. Fractures of the ZMC or ZA can often lead to unsightly malar depression, which should be corrected to restore normal facial contour and symmetry. Zygomaticomaxillary complex fractures can also cause significant functional issues, including trismus, enophthalmos, diplopia, and paresthesias of the infraorbital nerve. A suspected globe injury is a contraindication to operative management of ZMC fractures and should be addressed before any other surgical intervention is performed in the facial skeleton.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The zygoma contributes a significant portion of the inferior and lateral orbital walls. Any zygoma fracture warrants evaluating possible fractures or injury of the orbit, particularly open globe injuries. Cervical spine injury must be ruled out, as well as concomitant facial fractures, including frontal sinus, nasal, midface (eg, Lefort, naso-orbito-ethmoid), and mandible fractures. Midface trauma may result in nasal septal hematoma formation, which should be assessed and managed as necessary to avoid septal necrosis and perforation or saddle nose deformity. Lastly, malocclusion may result from mandibular or midfacial trauma and should be evaluated and addressed as well.

retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Fracture -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of a patient suffering from a maxillary fracture will require an interprofessional team approach. If there are facial lacerations, this may require closure from the oral and maxillofacial surgery team or the plastic surgery team. If there are concomitant injuries such as a limb or other fractures, the patient may require assessment and management from an orthopedic surgeon. One of the most significant consequences of a maxillary fracture is a retrobulbar hemorrhage, compression of the optic nerve, and loss of sight; therefore, assessment by an ophthalmology consultant is vital if there are any eye concerns. [10] Dento-alveolar injuries occur frequently with midface trauma, and these injuries should be assessed and managed by a dentist.

retrieved_docs.title: Le Fort Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: The supraorbital neurovascular bundle emerges from a notch or foramen situated in the midpupillary line of the superior orbital rim (see Image . Trigeminal Foramina of the Face), which should be protected to avoid postoperative hypesthesia of the forehead, even though scalp anesthesia posterior to the coronal incision is expected. If a periosteal flap is needed for frontal sinus obliteration or skull base reconstruction, it can be peeled off the underside of the coronal flap to provide the desired thickness of soft tissue, or it can be developed off the skull directly during coronal flap elevation (see Image . Pericranial Flap Elevation). Orbital access may also be required to reduce a Le Fort type III fracture, often via transconjunctival incisions to expose the orbital floor.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Operative Management
retrieved_docs.content: A key principle of facial fracture repair is a reduction with the fixation of fractures that involve the facial buttresses to restore the structural integrity of the midface. Several methods have evolved to repair ZA and ZMC fractures. However, closed reduction may be adequate for simple, low-velocity injuries of the ZA (see Type A1 below) that are nondisplaced or minimally displaced and remain stable after initial reduction. [5] Open reduction and internal fixation (see Image. ORIF of ZOMC Fracture) should be the approach of choice for fractures that are comminuted or are likely to be unstable after reduction. Typically, low-profile titanium mini-plates are used to fixate the fractured bone segments, working to repair fractures from laterally to medially and from stable to mobile segments. Absorbable plates may also be used for fixation in cases where follow-up is unlikely, but such plates should not be routinely used because absorbable plates are biomechanically weaker than titanium plates. [9] In any case, the conventional sequence of events involves surgical exposure of the displaced fracture site(s) as described below, reduction of the fractured bone segment(s), confirmation of alignment by direct visualization via the exposure obtained initially, and finally, rigid fixation. It is common practice among trauma surgeons to plate the zygomaticofrontal (ZF) suture first, then the zygomaticomaxillary (ZM) buttress or the infraorbital, and lastly, to repair the orbital floor, if necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Fracture -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The initial evaluation of the patient should be focused on immediate life support and securing the patient’s airway as facial trauma can commonly compromise the airway. It is also important to assess patients for potential cervical spine and head injuries, in particular, if the injury has been sustained from a high-velocity mechanism. Spinal fractures and dislocations have been associated with approximately 1.4% of Lefort fractures and 1% of spinal cord injuries. [10] A basic eye assessment is of paramount importance as maxillary fractures can result in severe ocular injuries, including retrobulbar hemorrhage, optic nerve compression, and retinal detachment. This can highlight any concerns that may require escalation to an opthalmologist for detailed assessment or clinical intervention. Up to 4.5% of ocular injuries caused by midface fractures require ophthalmologic surgery; therefore, evaluation of the eyes is prudent. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with mandibular fractures frequently have associated injuries. Initial treatment should be directed toward immediate, potentially life-threatening injuries such as airway obstruction, major hemorrhage, cervical spine injury, and intracranial injury. If oral intubation cannot be performed, indicated surgical airway may be required. Nasotracheal intubation should not be performed if midface, nasal, or anterior skull base fractures are suspected. Patients with mandibular fractures should observe cervical spine precautions until cleared. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery, Facial Laceration Repair -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Emergent injuries that require immediate attention, such as intracranial or intrathoracic trauma, take precedence over laceration repair. Securing the airway and maintaining hemodynamic stability remains the priority in a trauma evaluation. In a stable patient, head or facial lacerations should not be repaired until other bony or structural facial injuries have been assessed and the need for operative or specialty intervention has been determined. In some cases, facial lacerations provide optimal access to underlying bony injuries. Because the mechanisms of injury commonly resulting in facial lacerations include motor vehicle accidents, falls, and physical altercations, the cervical spine should always be evaluated and protected.

retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Fracture -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Maxillary fractures are one of the most common emergencies presenting in the acute setting. [1] Due to the complex anatomy within this region and the proximity to vital structures, including the brain, early diagnosis, and precise treatment planning are of paramount importance. Numerous treatment methods are well-practiced globally, and these aim to restore the patient’s quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Body Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical management can take place using an intraoral or extraoral approach, the choice of which depends mainly on the site and type of body fracture. Simple and fractures in the anterior segment with no or only slight dislocation should preferably have treatment using an intraoral approach. [15] This approach provides excellent access to the fracture site and allows observing the occlusion to reduce fracture and application of rigid fixation. The incision is placed in the vestibular region approximately 5 to 7 mm below the mucogingival junction to facilitate closure. This location also aids in the prevention of wound dehiscence. During an intraoral approach, care is necessary to avoid injuring the mental nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Sinus Fracture(Archived) -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Consultations vary depending on the involvement of maxilla and its surrounding structures: [10] Ophthalmology Otolaryngology Rhinology Facial plastic surgery Trauma surgery Oral maxillofacial surgery (OMFS) Neurosurgery

retrieved_docs.title: Le Fort Fractures -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with Le Fort fractures typically present to the emergency department, and consultation is usually requested from the facial trauma service, which may include otolaryngologists, plastic surgeons, facial plastic surgeons, and oral surgeons. If a periocular injury is present, an ophthalmology or oculoplastic surgery consult should be requested, and if a CSF leak, skull base, posterior table frontal sinus, or cranial vault fracture is identified, a neurosurgical opinion should be sought. Radiology will also become involved in the patient's care through diagnostic imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107
retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2615
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both specific subtypes of إصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Likewise, إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is classified as a form of إصابة داخل الجمجمة. Recognizing that these distinct injuries—fractures of the anterior and middle cranial fossae as well as closed head trauma—share the common characteristic of occurring within the skull underscores their relevance for accurate diagnosis, appropriate imaging, and targeted management in patients with intracranial trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both classified as إِصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Each represents a specific type of skull‑base fracture, with the anterior fossa fracture involving the front portion of the cranial base and the middle fossa fracture affecting the central region of the cranial base. Both conditions fall under the broader category of injuries inside the skull, highlighting their shared nature as intracranial traumatic lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the knowledge network unifies head and neck trauma, intracranial and skull‑base injuries, all skeletal fractures, their associated tissue damage, poisoning mechanisms, external causes (MVT, aircraft crash), procedural treatments, and the spectrum of late‑effect sequelae under the central concepts of fracture and late effects of unspecified injury, providing a comprehensive framework for injury classification, management, and long‑term patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: diverse injury types (intracranial, skeletal, facial), their specific anatomical subcategories, corresponding treatment modalities, and long‑term sequelae are interrelated, while also incorporating psychosocial determinants such as aggression that may influence injury occurrence and outcome. This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1038
retrieved_docs.content: Antemortem injury is classified within the broader category “Circumstances Related to Injury or Death.” Within this category, the specific circumstances “الاعتداء” (assault) and “تشويه الذات” (self‑inflicted injury) are listed as child entities. Both “تشويه الذات” and the intracranial condition “إصابة داخل الجمجمة” (injury inside the skull) are linked to “جرح” (wound), indicating that self‑harm and intracranial injuries often involve external wounds. Additionally, “Fracture of anterior fossa” and “Fracture of middle fossa” are each defined as types of “إصابة داخل الجمجمة,” specifying that fractures of the anterior and middle cranial fossae are particular forms of intracranial injury. This hierarchy clarifies how head‑related injuries are organized by cause (assault, self‑harm) and by anatomical subtype, and it highlights the common association of these injuries with wounds, which is important for forensic assessment and clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
retrieved_docs.content: Various late effects of bone injuries are directly linked to the underlying fracture. The “Late effect of fracture of multiple bones,” the “Late gevolgen van fractuur van schedel en aangezichtsbeenderen” (late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones), the Arabic phrases “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية” (late effects of fracture of the lower limbs), “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية” (late effects of fracture of the upper limbs), and “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق فخذ” (late effects of fracture of the femoral neck) are all associated with “كسر,” the Arabic term for fracture. This relationship underscores that regardless of the anatomical region—whether multiple bones, the skull and facial skeleton, the upper or lower extremities, or the femoral neck—post‑traumatic sequelae are fundamentally connected to the original fracture event, highlighting the importance of monitoring and managing these late complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1010
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, the late effects of fracture of the lower extremities, the late effects of fracture of the upper extremities, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these concepts illustrate that both the various delayed sequelae of fractures in different anatomical regions and specific surgical procedures for fracture management are intrinsically linked to the underlying fracture pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2619
retrieved_docs.content: Various late effects and a specific surgical procedure are directly linked to the condition of fracture (كسر). The “Late effect of fracture of multiple bones” is associated with fracture, as is the “Late effect of fracture of spine AND/OR trunk without spinal cord lesion.” The Dutch‑language entity “Late gevolgen van fractuur van schedel en aangezichtsbeenderen” (late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones) is likewise associated with fracture. In Arabic, both “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية” (late effects of fracture of the lower extremities) and “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق عظم الفخذ” (late effects of fracture of the femoral neck) are associated with fracture. Additionally, the procedure described as “Open reduction of open shoulder dislocation with fracture of greater tuberosity” represents a direct morphological manifestation of fracture. Together, these relationships illustrate that chronic sequelae of fractures across multiple anatomical regions and certain operative interventions are fundamentally connected to the underlying bone fracture, emphasizing the need to recognize fracture as the primary pathology when assessing late complications or planning surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2301
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck injury is linked to a broader category of intracranial injury (إصابة داخل الجمجمة). Within this category, a fracture of the anterior fossa and a fracture of the middle fossa are each classified as specific types of intracranial injury, highlighting the hierarchical relationship among head and neck trauma, general intracranial damage, and distinct skull‑base fracture subtypes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 87
retrieved_docs.content: neurovascular pathophysiology, CSF abnormalities, traumatic head injury, and related cranial nerve disorders, highlighting the importance of accurate classification, targeted procedural planning, and appropriate pharmacologic therapy for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 799
retrieved_docs.content: Antemortem injury is conceptually linked to an Unspecified site injury, indicating that injuries occurring before death can be categorized when the exact anatomical location is not defined. This Unspecified site injury is considered a broader form of intracranial injury and is primarily derived from the Arabic term “إصابات متعددة” (multiple injuries), reflecting that it often represents a collection of diverse traumatic findings. Antemortem injury also encompasses the category Circumstances Related to Injury or Death, which further subdivides into specific contexts such as “الاعتداء” (assault) and “تشويه الذات” (self‑harm). The self‑harm context is associated with the presence of a wound (“جرح”), highlighting the direct tissue damage that can result from intentional self‑inflicted injury. Within the spectrum of intracranial injuries, the detailed diagnosis “Closed fracture of vault of skull with cerebral laceration AND/OR contusion” is classified under two parent categories: “كسر في الجمجمة” (skull fracture) and intracranial injury. This dual classification underscores that the condition involves both a structural fracture of the skull vault and accompanying brain tissue injury, such as laceration or contusion. Together, these relationships illustrate how antemortem injuries are organized from broad, unspecified categories to specific clinical entities, and how the circumstances surrounding the injury—whether assault, self‑harm, or other mechanisms—inform the type and extent of tissue damage observed. Understanding these hierarchical and contextual links aids clinicians in accurately documenting trauma, assessing risk factors, and planning appropriate medical and forensic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2464
retrieved_docs.content: The condition “Closed fracture of vault of skull with cerebral laceration AND/OR contusion” is a specific subtype of “كسر في الجمجمة.” It denotes a closed fracture involving the cranial vault that is accompanied by a laceration or contusion of the brain tissue, and it is classified under the broader category of skull fractures. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians accurately categorize the injury, anticipate associated intracranial damage, and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: fractures), and الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق عظم الفخذ (femoral‑neck fractures). Morphologically, fracture is associated with كسر في الفقرة الرقبية (cervical vertebra fracture) and كسر في قبو القحف (fracture of the cranial vault); Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion is a specific form of كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, and “other fractures” further classifies cervical vertebral fractures. The direct_morphology_of relationship connects treatment; facial fracture or dislocation to كسر, indicating that management of facial fractures/dislocations is intrinsically tied to the underlying fracture pathology. Neck injury (إصابة في الرقبة) is a specific form of كسر في الفقرة الرقبية and, together with head and neck injury, resides under a broader injury hierarchy in which head and neck injury is the parent of neck injury. Pelvic fracture (كسر الحوض) frequently co‑occurs with cervical vertebra fracture, reflecting a pattern of multiple skeletal injuries. An unspecified fracture of the skull or

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck injury is a broad clinical category that includes intracranial injury, of which closed head injury is a specific type, and skull‑base fractures such as fracture of the anterior fossa and fracture of the middle fossa. Intracranial injury and skull‑base fractures are subclasses of fracture, the parent concept for all skeletal breakages. Fracture also encompasses cervical vertebral fracture (cervical spine fracture with cord lesion), cervical vertebral fracture as a subtype of neck injury, and pelvic fracture, which frequently co‑occurs with cervical vertebral fractures. Cranial‑vault fracture is the parent of unspecified skull or facial bone fracture, which is located in the facial bones (obličejové kosti) and is linked to facial‑bone procedural interventions (treatment of facial fracture or dislocation). Fracture of the femoral neck, fracture of the greater tuberosity, and fractures of the lower‑limb, upper‑limb, and multiple bones are all direct descendants of the general
